app.name: ChatGPT (Advanced Data Analysis)
app.bestFor: The default for ad-hoc analysis on an uploaded file — writes and runs Python, charts, and explains results conversationally.
app.rankBasis: Appears in all three cited AI data-analysis roundups and is the only top-ranked general assistant here with a built-in Python execution environment for uploaded files.
app.pricingFree: Free plan includes limited file uploads and data analysis
app.pricingPaid: ChatGPT Plus $20/month — expanded uploads, data analysis, and model access
pros: Reviewers consistently praise the low learning curve for ad-hoc analysis
Combines code generation, execution, charts, and explanation in one conversation
Useful across many file formats without a separate BI setup
cons: Generated analysis still requires validation for statistical and business correctness
Upload, context, and usage limits constrain large or repeated workloads
Less reproducible and governable than a shared notebook or BI project

app.name: Julius AI
app.bestFor: Conversational analysis of spreadsheets and CSVs with expert-level insights and visualizations in seconds.
app.rankBasis: Included in all three cited spreadsheet-analysis roundups and repeatedly positioned as a purpose-built conversational analyst rather than a general chatbot.
app.pricingFree: Free plan with a limited monthly message allowance
app.pricingPaid: Paid individual and team plans available; current allowance and billing price are shown at checkout
pros: Users value getting useful charts and explanations without writing code
Purpose-built workflow is simpler than prompting a general assistant
Fast for exploratory questions on ordinary spreadsheets
cons: Message limits can interrupt iterative analysis
Results still need checking when source data is messy or ambiguous
Less suitable for governed, repeatable enterprise reporting

app.name: Hex
app.bestFor: AI-assisted notebooks for data teams that live in SQL and Python and want reproducible, shareable analysis.
app.rankBasis: Appears in all three cited 2026 data-analysis roundups and has substantial independent G2 review volume as a collaborative analytics workspace.
app.pricingFree: Free Community plan for small projects with limited editors and compute
app.pricingPaid: Team plan from $36/editor/month when billed annually — shared workspaces, scheduled runs, and additional compute
features: SQL, Python, and no-code notebook cells
pros: Reviewers praise the polished collaboration and presentation layer
Keeps analysis, narrative, and interactive outputs in one reproducible project
Strong fit for teams already working in SQL and Python
cons: Per-editor pricing becomes expensive for broad audiences
Compute and workspace limits matter on lower tiers
More platform setup than a file-upload assistant

app.name: Tableau (with Einstein/Pulse AI)
app.bestFor: Enterprise BI dashboards with natural-language querying when your data already lives in a governed warehouse.
app.rankBasis: Named by all three cited AI-analysis comparisons and backed by one of the largest enterprise BI review footprints on G2 and Gartner Peer Insights.
app.pricingPaid: Tableau Creator $75/user/month billed annually — Tableau Cloud/Server authoring, Desktop, and Prep Builder
pros: Review consensus highlights visualization depth and dashboard flexibility
Mature governance and sharing for large organizations
Large ecosystem of connectors, training, and implementation partners
cons: Licensing and administration are costly and complex
Authors face a meaningful learning curve for advanced dashboards
Performance tuning is often required on large or poorly modeled data

app.name: Microsoft Excel + Copilot
app.bestFor: Enterprise AI inside Excel itself — formulas, pivots, and trend explanations in plain English without leaving the sheet.
app.rankBasis: Included in both cited AI-spreadsheet roundups and benefits from Excel's dominant installed base, giving its in-grid Copilot workflow unmatched distribution.
app.pricingPaid: Microsoft 365 Copilot $30/user/month with annual commitment — requires an eligible Microsoft 365 business license
features: Copilot access inside the spreadsheet grid
pros: Users can keep established Excel files and workflows
Strong fit for organizations already governed through Microsoft 365
Reduces friction for formula writing and first-pass exploration
cons: Copilot requires an additional eligible subscription and can be costly at scale
Output quality depends heavily on clean tables and explicit prompts
Advanced spreadsheet logic still needs human review and Excel expertise

app.name: Quadratic
app.bestFor: An AI-native spreadsheet blending cells, Python, and SQL for analysts who want code and grid together.
app.rankBasis: Appears in both cited AI-spreadsheet guides and stands out as the only ranked grid that natively mixes cells, Python, SQL, and AI in one canvas.
app.pricingFree: Free personal plan with core spreadsheet, AI, Python, and SQL capabilities
app.pricingPaid: Team plans available with collaboration and administration features; no stable public entry price was verified
features: Python and SQL directly in cells
pros: Reviewers like the direct combination of spreadsheet interaction and code
Open-source client and modern interface appeal to technical analysts
Useful bridge between conventional sheets and notebooks
cons: Younger ecosystem and fewer enterprise controls than Excel or Tableau
Compatibility with complex Excel workbooks is limited
Team pricing and plan limits are not consistently published

app.name: Deepnote
app.bestFor: Collaborative AI notebooks for teams standardizing on Python-based analysis and reporting.
app.rankBasis: Appears in both cited 2026 AI-analysis roundups and has sustained independent review coverage as one of the better-known collaborative cloud notebooks.
app.pricingFree: Free plan for small teams with limited editors, projects, and compute
app.pricingPaid: Paid team plans use per-editor pricing with additional compute and collaboration limits; current price is shown by the vendor
pros: Reviewers frequently praise real-time collaboration and quick onboarding
Managed environment removes much notebook infrastructure work
Good presentation and commenting workflow for mixed teams
cons: Larger machines and sustained compute increase cost
Cloud-only workflow may not fit strict local-data requirements
Some users report weaker flexibility than a fully controlled Jupyter environment

skill.name: xlsx (Anthropic Agent Skill)
skill.bestFor: Anthropic's official spreadsheet skill (from the 163k-star anthropics/skills repo, used in production Claude) — reads, analyzes, and builds Excel with formulas, pivots, and charts; the recognized leader for AI file-based analysis.
skill.rankBasis: Ships in Anthropic's official skills repository, which the existing source set records at roughly 163k GitHub stars, and is the canonical spreadsheet skill used by Claude's file workflow.
pros: Official Anthropic implementation provides a strong reference workflow
Handles both analysis and production of deliverable workbooks
Plain skill instructions are inspectable and adaptable
cons: Requires a compatible agent runtime and spreadsheet tooling
Complex workbook fidelity still needs visual verification
Repository-level popularity does not measure adoption of this individual skill

skill.name: pandas
skill.bestFor: The de-facto foundation of data analysis in Python (49k★) — the DataFrame library that ChatGPT Advanced Data Analysis and nearly every AI analysis tool run under the hood.
skill.rankBasis: The existing GitHub sources place pandas at roughly 49k stars, and it remains the standard DataFrame dependency behind a large share of Python analysis and AI-generated analysis code.
pros: Very large ecosystem and extensive documentation
Integrates with nearly every Python data and ML library
Expressive API for tabular cleaning and transformation
cons: In-memory workloads can exhaust RAM on large datasets
API breadth and indexing behavior create a learning curve
Performance often trails columnar or distributed engines at scale

skill.name: Apache Superset
skill.bestFor: The most-starred open-source BI/exploration platform (74k★) — a self-hostable Tableau/Looker alternative for SQL-driven dashboards and ad-hoc data exploration.
skill.rankBasis: The existing sources record roughly 74k GitHub stars, making Superset the highest-starred full open-source BI platform in this category and a frequent open-source Tableau alternative.
pros: Broad visualization and SQL feature set without license fees
Scales to substantial enterprise deployments with the right infrastructure
Active Apache-governed project and connector ecosystem
cons: Installation, upgrades, and production operations require engineering effort
Nontechnical users face a steeper learning curve than simpler BI tools
Dashboard polish and workflow consistency can lag commercial suites

skill.name: Metabase
skill.bestFor: The easiest open-source BI tool to self-host (48k★) — no-SQL question builder plus full SQL for teams that want fast dashboards over their database.
skill.rankBasis: The existing sources place Metabase at roughly 48k GitHub stars, and independent open-source analytics comparisons consistently single it out for fast setup and no-SQL exploration.
skill.pricingPaid: Optional Metabase Cloud and commercial editions are available with hosted operations and enterprise controls
pros: Reviewers consistently cite ease of setup and approachable querying
Useful dashboards can be built quickly by nontechnical teams
Open-source edition covers a meaningful core feature set
cons: Advanced permissions, embedding, and governance require paid editions
Complex semantic modeling is less capable than enterprise BI suites
Large deployments require careful query and application tuning

skill.name: Project Jupyter (JupyterLab)
skill.bestFor: The universal notebook environment where interactive analysis actually happens (15k★, ACM Software System Award project) — Python/R/Julia cells, charts, and reproducible reports.
skill.rankBasis: The existing sources record roughly 15k GitHub stars, while Jupyter's ACM Software System Award and broad institutional adoption make it the category's established notebook standard.
features: Python, R, Julia, and other kernels
pros: Ubiquitous format with a very large scientific ecosystem
Excellent for exploratory and reproducible code-plus-narrative work
Runs locally, on servers, and through many managed services
cons: Hidden execution state can make notebooks hard to reproduce
Collaboration and deployment need additional infrastructure
Large notebooks become difficult to test, review, and maintain

skill.name: K-Dense scientific-agent-skills (EDA)
skill.bestFor: Automated exploratory-data-analysis and publication-quality visualization skills from the most-starred science skills library (31.6k★, 148 SKILL.md skills).
skill.rankBasis: The existing source set records roughly 31.6k stars for the broader K-Dense scientific skills library, the strongest adoption signal among specialist science and EDA skill collections here.
pros: Broad collection covers analysis beyond basic spreadsheet summaries
Skill files are transparent and easy to adapt
Strong community interest for a specialist scientific repository
cons: Quality and dependencies vary across the large skill collection
Requires an agent plus local scientific Python tooling
Repository stars do not establish validation of every statistical workflow

skill.name: PostHog
skill.bestFor: Open-source product analytics (37k★) — event data, funnels, session replay, and SQL when your analysis is about how users behave in a product.
skill.rankBasis: The existing GitHub source records roughly 37k stars, giving PostHog one of the largest open-source adoption signals for product-focused behavioral analysis.
skill.pricingPaid: Hosted PostHog Cloud offers usage-based pricing with monthly free allowances
pros: Combines several product-data tools in one platform
Generous cloud allowances and a self-host option lower adoption friction
Reviewers value rapid instrumentation and active product development
cons: Breadth makes the interface and configuration complex
High event volume can create significant hosting or cloud cost
Self-hosting the full stack is operationally demanding

skill.name: nimrodfisher/data-analytics-skills
skill.bestFor: A small, focused community collection of Claude skills for the everyday analytics workflow — cleaning, aggregation, and charting on tabular data. Low star count, so ranked low.
skill.rankBasis: A focused public repository for analytics skills, but its single GitHub source and low star count provide much thinner independent adoption evidence than the projects ranked above it.
pros: Small scope makes the skills easy to inspect
Targets common analyst tasks rather than a broad generic framework
Free to fork and customize
cons: Very limited community and independent review evidence
Maintenance continuity is uncertain for a small repository
Users must supply and validate the underlying analysis environment

skill.name: juanlurg/data-science-claude-skills
skill.bestFor: Environment-aware EDA skills (auto-eda, dataset-doctor) that detect your pandas/polars library and SQL dialect. Genuine but niche and low-star, so ranked low.
skill.rankBasis: The repository offers distinctive environment-aware EDA skills, but the cited GitHub and community coverage indicate niche adoption and a much smaller user signal than the leading projects.
pros: Environment awareness reduces generic or incompatible code suggestions
Focused dataset-doctor workflow is practical for early analysis
Skill definitions can be audited and modified
cons: Low star and review volume make reliability hard to assess
Depends on local libraries and agent execution quality
Narrower maintenance base than established data tooling

notes: Skill/Repo track is ranked by real quality, reputation, and adoption — not by form factor; kind (skill|repo) is only a tag. Anthropic's official xlsx skill leads for AI file-based analysis (it is the purpose-built, production-used equivalent of the app track's ChatGPT ADA), followed by the foundational open-source data stack pandas, Superset, Metabase, and Jupyter, then K-Dense's large EDA skill library. PostHog covers product analytics. Small community skill collections (nimrodfisher, juanlurg) are genuine but low-star, so they rank last at low confidence. All entries are real Agent Skills (SKILL.md) or notable open-source repos — no MCP servers. Star counts verified on GitHub. App leaders (ChatGPT, Julius, Hex) recur across most independent lists; several data-analysis ranking sites are vendor/affiliate blogs, so app consensus weights tools appearing across multiple independent sources.
